critic_raw: The design uses 'query-article metadata overlap (shared product area, compatible versions)' as a derived feature. For the dense embedding model, this overlap is used as an additional feature alongside embeddings. But BM25 also uses product area and version filtering. If the dense model's gains are primarily from better metadata matching (product area alignment) rather than semantic understanding, the metadata overlap feature would be the driver. The Spearman popularity correlation checks for popularity matching but does not check for metadata matching as the driver.
defender_raw: The metadata ablation concern is partially addressed by the design's framework: both models apply the same product area and version filtering as retrieval constraints. The metadata overlap feature is a soft alignment signal available to the dense model. If metadata overlap drives most of the NDCG@5 improvement, an ablation (dense model without metadata overlap feature) would reveal this. The design doesn't explicitly include this ablation, but the BM25-to-dense ranking overlap metric ('identifies whether the model learns distinct patterns or implicitly recovers lexical matching') partially addresses whether the dense model adds value beyond lexical + metadata matching. This is an honest assessment.
adjudication_raw: One concern: metadata overlap driving dense model gains rather than semantic matching. Partially addressed by BM25-to-dense overlap metric. No explicit metadata ablation but design framework has the tools to diagnose this. The absence of an explicit ablation is a minor gap but not a fundamental flaw. Verdict: defense_wins.
